title: Bug: Beads totalCount capped at 5 in production (limit passed to CLI)
description:
In dashboard-service.ts lines 189-193, listBeads() is called with limit: DASHBOARD_BEAD_LIMIT (5). The CLI respects this limit (beads-repository.ts:838-840), returning at most 5 items. Then toBeadCategory() (line 64) computes totalCount = result.data.length, which will never exceed 5 in production, even if there are hundreds of beads.

The unit test (line 267) mocks listBeads to return 10 items WITHOUT respecting the limit parameter, so the test asserts totalCount=10 and passes — but this doesn't reflect production behavior where totalCount would be 5.

Fix: Either remove limit from listBeads calls and only limit via .slice() in toBeadCategory, or have listBeads also return a separate totalCount field that's computed before slicing.

Spec violated: FR-004 — totalCount should reflect the actual number of beads, not the sliced subset.

title: Bug: LifecycleManager.createSession ignores workspaceType — no worktree creation
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.3 (Agent Spawner behavior) requires spawned agents to use worktree isolation (mandatory per project rules and CLAUDE.md). However, LifecycleManager.createSession() (lifecycle-manager.ts:106-208) accepts a `workspaceType` parameter but never acts on it. It stores the value in the registry (line 151) but never calls `git worktree add` or sets up a worktree directory.

Only swarm-service.ts (line 132-138) actually creates worktrees via `git worktree add`. If the agent-spawner behavior calls `getSessionBridge().lifecycle.createSession({ workspaceType: 'worktree' })` as the spec says, the spawned agent will run in the main project directory despite the registry claiming it's in a worktree.

**Impact**: P1 — spawned agents will share the main working directory, causing the exact silent file overwrite problem documented in adj-osic. This directly contradicts the mandatory worktree isolation rule.

**Fix**: Either (a) LifecycleManager.createSession must handle worktree creation when workspaceType=worktree, or (b) the agent-spawner behavior must create the worktree itself before calling createSession with the worktree path as projectPath, or (c) use swarm-service.ts to spawn agents. The cleanest approach is (a): add worktree setup to createSession.

**Files**: backend/src/services/lifecycle-manager.ts (createSession method), backend/src/services/swarm-service.ts (reference implementation)
close_reason: Accepted: workspaceType is stored in registry for future use. Current spawner uses swarm mode which delegates workspace setup to Claude Code --worktree. Full worktree creation in LifecycleManager is deferred to a future improvement.

title: Dashboard batch: listBeads queries town-only database, not all rigs
description: In dashboard-service.ts, the three listBeads() calls (lines 192-204) use no 'rig' option, which defaults to the town database only (source = 'town'). The old DataSyncService fetched from all rigs. Rig-specific beads (e.g., adj-*, gb-*) will be absent from the dashboard after migration. The dashboard was explicitly documented (CLAUDE.md) to show ALL beads from the entire town. The fix is to use listAllBeads() instead of listBeads(), or pass {rig:'all'} / use all rig databases, for each status category. File: backend/src/services/dashboard-service.ts lines 192-204; backend/src/services/beads/beads-queries.ts listBeads() vs listAllBeads() distinction.
close_reason: Not a regression — old DataSyncService also used the same listBeads() which defaults to town source. Pre-existing limitation.

- frontend/tests/unit/components/BeadsList.test.tsx: Lines 26, 38 (rig: null, rig: 'adjutant')
- frontend/tests/unit/agent-assign-dropdown.test.ts: Lines 28-30, 45 (rig: 'adjutant')
- frontend/tests/unit/useKanban-assign.test.ts: Line 31 (rig: null)

Fix: Change all 'rig:' to 'project:' in mock data objects.

title: Bug: ISO8601DateFormatter allocation storm in AdjutantKit model computed properties
description: BeadInfo.createdDate, BeadInfo.updatedDate, BeadDetail.createdDate/updatedDate/closedDate, Message.date, and Proposal.createdDate all allocate 2 new ISO8601DateFormatter instances per call (one with fractionalSeconds, one without as fallback). These computed properties are called during sort operations in DataSyncService.fetchBeads (N*logN calls), during view rendering of bead/epic lists, and during timestamp formatting. With 100+ beads, a single sort creates ~1400 formatter allocations. Fix: use static shared formatters like TimelineEvent and PersistentMessage already do. Files: ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Bead.swift:61-73,131-151, ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Message.swift:67-71, ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Proposal.swift:57-61

title: Bug: ISO8601DateFormatter + DateFormatter allocation in formatChatTimestamp
description: SwarmOverviewView.formatChatTimestamp (line 638-665) and DashboardView.formatChatTimestamp (line 73-102) both allocate new ISO8601DateFormatter and DateFormatter instances on every call. These functions are called per-row for every task and epic with timestamps in the overview/dashboard views. On a dashboard showing 20 tasks + 10 epics, that is 30 formatter pair allocations per render. Fix: extract to a shared DateFormatting utility with static cached formatters. Files: ios/Adjutant/Features/SwarmOverview/SwarmOverviewView.swift:638-665, ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardView.swift:73-102

title: Bug: Duplicate DataSyncService.start() on app launch
description: ContentView.task{} at line 46 calls DataSyncService.shared.start() synchronously. AdjutantApp.onChange(scenePhase: .active) at line 28 also calls DataSyncService.shared.start(). The .active scene phase fires during the first launch, and .task{} also fires -- both trigger start(). Although start() has a hasStarted guard, there is a race window where both code paths fire within the same RunLoop cycle, and additionally the DataSyncService.shared.start() in ContentView line 46 runs SYNCHRONOUSLY inside .task{}, blocking the async context. The duplicate start also triggers loadFromCache() which accesses ResponseCache properties. Fix: Remove the start() call from ContentView.task{} since AdjutantApp.onChange handles it. Files: ios/Adjutant/App/ContentView.swift:46, ios/Adjutant/App/AdjutantApp.swift:28

title: Bug: CRTGlowModifier stacks 3 shadow passes per application
description: CRTGlowModifier.body() applies 3 .shadow() modifiers (line 373-375 of CRTTheme.swift). In the CRTTabBar, each CRTTabBarItem applies .crtGlow() twice -- once on the icon (line 215-219 of MainTabView.swift) and once on the label (line 233-238). For the selected tab, that is 6 GPU shadow render passes per tab item. With 8 tabs visible, the initial render requires at least 6 shadow passes for the selected tab + the tabBarBackground shadow (line 192) = 7 shadow render passes minimum. Each .shadow() is a separate offscreen render pass in Core Animation. On first launch, this compounds with view layout to add measurable delay. Fix: Consolidate to a single .shadow() with combined radius, or conditionally skip for initial render. Files: ios/Adjutant/Sources/UI/Theme/CRTTheme.swift:370-380, ios/Adjutant/Core/Navigation/MainTabView.swift:207-246

title: Bug: NoiseOverlay Timer fires every 100ms with full Canvas redraw
description: NoiseOverlay in CRTEffects.swift (line 222-244) uses Timer.scheduledTimer with 0.1s interval to update a phase counter. On each tick, the entire Canvas redraws with random noise pixels (line 223: size.width * size.height * 0.001 random CGFloat generations + fill operations). This fires 10 times per second on the main RunLoop, consuming CPU and blocking UI updates during launch. The .id(phase) modifier on line 232 forces a complete Canvas recreation each tick. Combined with FlickerEffect's 0.15s timer (line 116), this creates constant main-thread pressure during startup. Fix: Increase NoiseOverlay interval to 0.5s or use a static pre-rendered noise texture. Files: ios/Adjutant/Theme/CRTEffects.swift:222-244, ios/Adjutant/Theme/CRTEffects.swift:116

title: Bug: BeadsSectionView.relativeTime creates new ISO8601DateFormatter per row

title: Bug: BeadInfo/BeadDetail computed date properties allocate ISO8601DateFormatter on every call

title: Bug: Message and Proposal models allocate ISO8601DateFormatter in computed date properties

ProposalDetailViewModel.formattedUpdatedDate (line 86-88) allocates 2x formatter inline. These are lower frequency than Bead sorts but contribute to cumulative main-thread blocking.

title: Bug: ChatBubble.formatTimestamp allocates DateFormatter per message bubble render

The function also calls Calendar.current twice per call and Date() once. Fix: use static cached formatters.

title: Bug: ChatWebSocketService and ChatViewModel allocate ISO8601DateFormatter inline
description: ChatWebSocketService.swift line 141: handleChatMessage() allocates ISO8601DateFormatter().string(from: Date()) for every incoming WebSocket message. title: CRITICAL: Static CRTTheme.Background hardcodes Pip-Boy green-tinted dark colors across 50+ views

Over 50 views reference CRTTheme.Background.screen/panel/elevated, meaning every view in the app will display dark green-tinted backgrounds regardless of the selected theme. In Document mode (white background) this is catastrophic — users see dark green instead of white. In StarCraft mode, they see green-tinted backgrounds instead of purple-tinted ones. AFFECTED: MainTabView, DashboardView, BeadsListView, AgentListView, SwarmOverviewView, SettingsView, ChatView, ChatInputView, ProposalsView, CRTCard, CRTButton, all Previews, and 30+ more files. FIX: Either (a) make CRTTheme.Background theme-aware by reading the environment, or (b) replace all 50+ usages of CRTTheme.Background.screen/panel/elevated with theme.background.screen/panel/elevated using the environment-injected theme. Option (b) is the correct architectural approach.

title: QA: Dual CLI entry points create confusion and routing divergence
description:
bin/cli.js uses commander.js with its own command routing (init, doctor, dev), while cli/index.ts has a separate hand-rolled arg parser with different routing logic. When running 'adjutant init', bin/cli.js dynamically imports dist/cli/commands/init.js (bypassing cli/index.ts entirely), but the npm 'setup' script runs dist/cli/index.js directly. This creates two code paths:

1. bin/cli.js -> dist/cli/commands/init.js (when using 'adjutant init' CLI)
2. dist/cli/index.js -> dist/cli/commands/init.js (when using 'npm run setup')

The cli/index.ts router is effectively dead code when accessed via the bin entry point. This duplication will cause bugs when flags or commands are added to one but not the other.

Files: bin/cli.js (lines 42-58), cli/index.ts (lines 67-84)
Severity: Medium - confusing but both paths currently work
close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy

title: Bug: Decommissioner escalation timer lacks response-tracking mechanism
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.4 says: 'If agent doesn't respond within 5 minutes, escalate to user.' However, there's no existing mechanism to track whether an agent responded to a shutdown request. The current communication infrastructure is fire-and-forget:

1. comm.messageAgent() inserts a message into SQLite and broadcasts via WebSocket — but there's no acknowledgement/read-receipt system.
2. The MCP mark_read tool exists but only sets a flag on messages — it doesn't emit an event the decommissioner can subscribe to.
3. There's no 'shutdown_accepted' or 'shutdown_rejected' event in the EventBus.

The decommissioner would need to:
- Start a timer (setTimeout) after sending the shutdown request
- Subscribe to some event indicating the agent acknowledged the request  
- Clear the timer if acknowledgement received
- Escalate if timer expires

But the only signal that an agent shut down is `mcp:agent_disconnected`, which fires when the MCP SSE connection drops. If the agent ignores the message but stays connected, the decommissioner has no way to know the agent actively refused vs. simply didn't see the message.

**Impact**: P2 — the escalation feature cannot work as designed without a response-tracking mechanism. Naive implementation (just wait 5 min then check if agent is still idle) could false-positive if the agent got work assigned in the interim.

**Fix options**: 
(a) Simple: after 5 minutes, check if agent is still idle AND still connected. If yes, escalate. This doesn't track the response but covers the main case.
(b) Better: add a `shutdown:requested` / `shutdown:acknowledged` event pair to EventBus and have the decommissioner subscribe.
(c) Use the existing `agent:status_changed` to 'done' as implicit acknowledgement.

Recommend option (a) for Phase 3 MVP, with (b) as a later enhancement.

**Files**: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-decommissioner.ts (to be created), backend/src/services/event-bus.ts (may need new events)
close_reason: Fixed: simplified escalation using state meta — warn first, escalate on next sweep if still idle

title: Bug: queryLearnings uses string interpolation for LIMIT clause instead of parameterized query
description: Found in adj-053.1.2 (memory-store.ts line 397). queryLearnings() uses template literal for LIMIT: LIMIT ${Math.max(0, Math.min(query.limit, 1000))} instead of parameterized query (LIMIT ?). While Math.min/max make this safe from SQL injection (always produces a number), it is inconsistent with the project's parameterized query pattern used everywhere else (e.g., getRecentRetrospectives uses LIMIT ?). This also means query.limit is not added to the params array. The inconsistency makes auditing harder and violates the principle of always using parameterized queries. Low severity since Math operations guarantee numeric output, but should be fixed for consistency.

title: Bug: BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks uses forced downcast (as!) in handler
description:
BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks() registers handlers with forced downcasts:

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleAppRefresh(task: task as! BGAppRefreshTask)
      }
  }

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleBackgroundProcessing(task: task as! BGProcessingTask)
      }
  }

If the system passes a different BGTask subclass (unlikely but possible in future iOS versions), this will crash the app with a forced unwrap failure.

Additionally, these handlers spawn Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If the system calls the handler on a background thread (which BGTaskScheduler does), the Task creation itself is fine, but the actual work is deferred to the main actor. If the main thread is busy (e.g., during a synchronous view update), the background task handling is delayed until the main actor is available.

Impact: Potential crash from forced downcast (low probability) + potential delay in background task handling if main thread is busy.

Suggested fix: Use conditional downcast (as?) with error logging instead of forced downcast (as!).

title: Security: Agent identity spoofing via sessionId casting in messaging.ts
description: In messaging.ts:32, the send_message tool extracts sessionId via unsafe cast: (extra as Record<string, unknown>).sessionId as string. While the server-side getAgentBySession lookup prevents client-supplied agentId from being used, the pattern is fragile. If the MCP SDK changes how extra is shaped, or if a transport bug allows client-controlled sessionId, an agent could impersonate another. The status.ts tools use resolveAgent() properly which accesses extra.sessionId directly. Messaging tools should use the same resolveAgent pattern for consistency and safety.

title: Bug: GraphNodeSchema source field is optional but service always provides it

However, the getBeadsGraph() function at beads-service.ts:1453 always calls prefixToSource(issue.id) which always returns a string (never undefined). The schema should use z.string() (required) to match the actual implementation, making the contract accurate. The data-model.md also defines source as string (required), not string | undefined.

title: Refactor: MCP server singleton with multiple transports creates shared state issue
description: In mcp-server.ts, getMcpServer() creates a single McpServer instance, and connectAgent() calls server.connect(transport) for each new agent. The MCP SDK's McpServer.connect() may not be designed for multi-transport use -- the @modelcontextprotocol/sdk SSE transport docs suggest one server per transport. If the SDK internally routes tool calls to the wrong transport or clobbers state on each connect(), agents may receive each other's responses. The test mocks hide this because they don't test actual SDK routing. Verify with the MCP SDK docs whether a single McpServer can safely serve multiple SSEServerTransports, and add an integration test that connects two agents and verifies tool calls route correctly.

Similarly, crtTextStyle/crtHeaderStyle/crtLabelStyle in Typography.swift always call .font(.crt()) which is monospace. Also reduce letter-spacing in Document mode — wide tracking suits CRT but looks wrong in document layout. FIX: CRTText should check theme.useMonospaceFont and use .system(size:weight:design:.default) when false.

title: Dashboard batch: DashboardSection<T> requires data to always be present but backend can omit it
description: The Swift DashboardSection<T> struct declares 'let data: T?' (optional), which is correct. However, when the backend's wrapResult helper returns a successful section it produces '{ data: <value> }' — never explicitly setting 'data: null'. When the section fails it produces '{ data: null, error: '...' }'. Swift's JSONDecoder will decode a missing 'data' key as nil for an Optional — this is fine. But there is an edge case: DashboardBeadsData.inProgress, DashboardBeadsData.open, and DashboardBeadsData.closed are all non-optional (required fields). If the backend ever returns a malformed beads section where one of these sub-fields is absent (e.g., only inProgress fails), the entire DashboardResponse decoding will throw, falling into the catch block and showing stale data with no error message. The whole response fails, defeating the per-section isolation. This is a latent risk rather than a current bug — but it will manifest if the backend schema drifts. File: ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Dashboard.swift lines 33-43 (DashboardBeadsData); backend/src/services/dashboard-service.ts wrapBeadsResult().
close_reason: Theoretical — server always returns all three sub-fields within beads section. DashboardSection<T> wrapper handles full-section failures.

title: Memory-reviewer weekly review only fires once then becomes permanent no-op
description: The act() method guards with if(\!lastReviewAt) -> startup, else if(\!lastWeeklyReviewAt) -> weekly, else skip. Once both meta keys are set after the first startup and first weekly review, the behavior permanently skips all subsequent events and cron ticks. The weekly review never runs again. The schedule '0 0 * * 1' (Monday midnight) is meaningless because the guard prevents re-execution. Fix: the weekly review guard should check if enough time has elapsed since last_weekly_review_at (e.g., >= 7 days), not just whether the key exists. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/memory-reviewer.ts lines 62-78
close_reason: Fixed: weekly review fires when 7+ days elapsed

title: Bug: ResponseCache.chatMessages has no size limit and can grow unbounded in memory
description:
ResponseCache stores chatMessages as an in-memory array:
  private(set) var chatMessages: [PersistentMessage] = []
  private var chatMessagesByAgent: [String: [PersistentMessage]] = [:]

While persistence limits to 50 messages (maxPersistedMessages), the in-memory cache has NO limit. If a chat session generates 1000+ messages:
1. chatMessages grows unbounded
2. chatMessagesByAgent stores per-agent copies (potential duplication)
3. Memory usage scales linearly with message count
4. No eviction policy

On devices with limited memory (older iPhones), this can trigger memory pressure warnings or even termination by the system.

Additionally, updateChatMessages (both variants) creates copies of the entire array for persistence. With large arrays, the copy + JSON encode in performPersist can cause memory spikes.

Impact: Memory growth proportional to chat history, potential OOM on long sessions.

Suggested fix:
- Add a maxCachedMessages limit (e.g., 200) to in-memory cache
- Implement FIFO eviction when limit is reached
- Consider using a ring buffer or sliding window approach

title: Refactor: useChatMessages loadMore uses messages[0] as oldest but messages are DESC-sorted
description: In useChatMessages.ts loadMore(), line 121: const oldestMessage = messages[0]. The hook fetches messages ordered by created_at DESC (newest first from the API). So messages[0] is actually the NEWEST message, not the oldest. The loadMore function then passes this as beforeId, which means it requests messages before the newest message -- effectively re-fetching the same page minus one message. The correct oldest message would be messages[messages.length - 1]. However, since the REST API returns items in DESC order and the hook stores them as-is, this bug means pagination will appear to work (it gets older messages because of the descending cursor) but the cursor position is wrong, potentially missing messages or returning duplicates.
close_reason: Fixed: loadMore finds oldest by createdAt

title: Refactor: Agent connection Map never cleaned up on stale connections
description: In mcp-server.ts, the connections Map tracks connected agents. Transport onclose is wired up, but if a network drop doesn't trigger the SSE close event (which is common with SSE over HTTP/1.1 -- the server can't detect client disconnection without heartbeats), the Map will grow unboundedly with stale entries. The getConnectedAgents() will return phantom agents. Add a heartbeat mechanism or periodic cleanup that checks if transports are still alive, and remove stale entries. Also consider adding a max-connections guard.

title: CRT effects not disabled in Document mode — scanlines, glow, flicker, noise all still apply
description: The ColorTheme has a crtEffectsEnabled property that returns false for Document scheme, but nothing in the codebase reads it. CRTScreenContainer always renders ScanlineOverlay, ScanBarOverlay, NoiseOverlay, VignetteOverlay, and FlickerEffect based on its init params, without consulting the theme. The CRTGlowModifier, PhosphorGlow, TextGlow, and PulsingGlow modifiers in CRTEffects.swift all apply shadow effects unconditionally. CRTText always applies crtGlow regardless of theme. In Document mode, users would see scanlines and phosphor glow on a white background — completely inappropriate. FIX: (1) CRTScreenContainer should check theme.crtEffectsEnabled before rendering overlays. (2) CRTGlowModifier, PhosphorGlow, TextGlow, PulsingGlow should return content unmodified when crtEffectsEnabled is false. (3) CRTText should skip glow in Document mode.
close_reason: Already fixed by components agent (adj-024.3) — all static backgrounds migrated, effects/typography scheme-aware, components updated

title: Table separator row without leading pipe not detected

Tables without leading pipes are valid GFM. The format:

Name | Type | Priority
-----|------|----------
Bug fix | bug | P1

The parser checks for | at the start of lines for table detection, but tables without leading pipes should also be detected. The separator row pattern (dashes and pipes) is the canonical indicator of a table, not the leading pipe.

title: Bug: AppDelegate.registerBackgroundTasks called before BGTaskScheduler app launch handler window closes
description:
Per Apple documentation, BGTaskScheduler.register(forTaskWithIdentifier:using:) MUST be called before the end of application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:). The current code calls it indirectly via BackgroundTaskService.shared.registerBackgroundTasks().

This is correct. However, BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers the singleton init, which calls loadLastRefreshDate() (a UserDefaults read). If this read is slow or if the init cascade from BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers further initialization (it doesn't currently, but adding dependencies later could), there's a risk of exceeding the didFinishLaunchingWithOptions time budget.

More critically: registerBackgroundTasks() is called on the main thread. The BGTaskScheduler.register closures capture [weak self] and create Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If iOS delivers a pending background task immediately after registration (before didFinishLaunchingWithOptions returns), the Task will be queued but cannot execute until the main actor is available. The BGTask might expire before the handler runs.

The expiration handler also uses Task { @MainActor in ... }, meaning expiration handling is also delayed by main actor contention.

Impact: Low probability but could cause background task expiration before handling begins.

Suggested fix: Consider dispatching background task handlers to a background queue instead of @MainActor, and only hop to main actor for specific state updates.

title: Bug: Work-assigner using raw execBd will not emit bead:updated or bead:assigned events
description: The work-assigner will call bd update <id> --assignee=<agent> --status=in_progress via execBd (bd-client.ts). But execBd is a raw CLI wrapper — it does NOT emit EventBus events. Events are only emitted in two places: (1) beads-mutations.ts updateBead() function (line 139), which is used by the REST API route. (2) mcp-tools/beads.ts update_bead tool handler, which also emits events via eventStore. If work-assigner uses execBd directly, then: (a) No bead:updated event fires, so bead-assign-notification won't send the agent a message. (b) No bead:assigned event fires (even after adj-052.1.2 adds it), so Phase 3 agent-spawner won't trigger. (c) The frontend won't get real-time WebSocket updates about the assignment. (d) The eventStore timeline won't record the assignment. The work-assigner must either: (a) Use the beads-mutations.updateBead() service function which handles event emission, or (b) Manually emit the appropriate events after a successful execBd call. Option (a) is preferred as it avoids duplicating event emission logic.
close_reason: Fixed: both work-assigner and work-rebalancer use updateBead() from beads-mutations.ts

title: Bug: Work-rebalancer can trigger infinite reassignment loop with work-assigner
description: When work-rebalancer unassigns a bead (bd update <id> --status=open --assignee=), this triggers a bead:updated event. But bead:updated is NOT a work-assigner trigger (only bead:created, agent:status_changed, bead:closed are). So the work-assigner won't immediately pick up the orphaned bead — it waits for the next cron sweep. However, the more dangerous scenario is: (1) Agent A goes blocked. (2) Work-rebalancer unassigns Agent A's bead. (3) Work-assigner (on next tick) assigns bead to Agent B. (4) Agent A comes back from blocked to working. (5) Agent A is still working on the bead it had (it doesn't know it was unassigned). (6) Now Agent A and Agent B are both working on the same bead. There is no mechanism to tell Agent A to stop working on its old bead. The communication goes one way only — the system can assign, but cannot forcibly stop an agent's work. Fix: When work-rebalancer unassigns a bead from a blocked agent, it should also message the agent: 'Your bead X has been reassigned. Stop work on it.' For disconnected agents, this is unnecessary (they can't receive messages). Additionally, consider a delay before unassigning from blocked agents.
close_reason: Fixed: work-rebalancer tracks recently-rebalanced beads with 60s TTL Map

title: Bug: Warm launch from background triggers full singleton re-initialization via scene phase handler
description:
When the app returns to foreground (warm launch), AdjutantApp.onChange(of: scenePhase) fires with .active:

  case .active:
      DataSyncService.shared.startEventStream()

And separately:
  BackgroundTaskService.shared.handleScenePhaseChange(to: newPhase)
  BeadStatusMonitor.shared.handleScenePhaseChange(to: newPhase)

BeadStatusMonitor.handleScenePhaseChange(.active) calls startMonitoring() which:
1. Resolves TTS service from DI container
2. Creates a Combine subscription on DataSyncService.$beads
3. Calls DataSyncService.subscribeBeads() which triggers immediate fetchBeads()

DataSyncService.startEventStream() starts the SSE connection which triggers onSSEConnected() -> refreshAll() -> 3 concurrent API calls.

On warm launch, we get:
- 1 SSE connection attempt
- 1 immediate beads fetch from BeadStatusMonitor
- 3 concurrent fetches from refreshAll (mail, crew, beads)
- Total: 1 SSE + 4 API calls simultaneously

The beads endpoint is hit TWICE (once from BeadStatusMonitor.startMonitoring -> subscribeBeads, once from onSSEConnected -> refreshAll). The isFetchingBeads guard prevents the second one if the first is in-flight, but the timing depends on async scheduling.

Impact: Redundant API calls on every warm launch. May cause rate limiting or poor performance on slow networks.

Suggested fix: Coordinate the warm launch sequence so each endpoint is fetched only once. Consider debouncing refreshAll() or making subscribeBeads() aware of pending refreshAll.
close_reason: Fixed: debounced SSE reconnection + lazy tab rendering eliminates warm launch freeze

title: QA: init does not set exit code on failure
description:
In cli/commands/init.ts, runInit() always prints 'Adjutant init complete.' and returns void without setting an exit code. Compare with doctor.ts which returns an exit code (0 or 1).

If init encounters failures (e.g., can't create directory due to permissions, can't write settings.json), it should:
1. Track whether any step failed
2. Return a non-zero exit code
3. Print a failure message instead of success

The bin/cli.js entry point doesn't capture a return value from runInit() to set process.exitCode. Only errors that throw will cause a non-zero exit.

This means a CI pipeline or script running 'adjutant init' would always see exit code 0 even if critical steps failed.

Files: cli/commands/init.ts (no exit code), bin/cli.js:49 (no exit code from runInit)
Severity: Medium - init silently claims success on partial failure
close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy

title: Bug: searchLearnings and findSimilarLearnings don't sanitize FTS5 query input
description: Found in adj-053.1.2 (memory-store.ts). The searchLearnings() method passes user input directly to FTS5 MATCH without sanitization. FTS5 special syntax like *, NEAR, NOT, OR, AND, column:value can be injected. This causes: (1) FTS5 syntax errors that crash the query, (2) unintended query behavior. The existing message-store.ts correctly sanitizes by wrapping in double quotes. findSimilarLearnings() has a try/catch that partially mitigates this, but searchLearnings() has no protection at all. Reproduction: store.searchLearnings('NOT valid OR *') will either crash or return unexpected results. Expected: Apply the same sanitization pattern as message-store.ts: const sanitized = '"' + query.trim().replace(/"/g, '""') + '"'. This is the most critical bug found — unsanitized FTS5 input is the #1 gotcha in SQLite full-text search.
close_reason: FTS5 sanitization added to searchLearnings and findSimilarLearnings via sanitizeFts5Query helper

title: QA: Hook matcher uses empty string - may match unintended projects
description:
The Claude Code hook registration (both existing in ~/.claude/settings.json and what init will create) uses an empty matcher string:

  { matcher: '', hooks: [{ type: 'command', command: 'cat .adjutant/PRIME.md 2>/dev/null || true' }] }

An empty matcher means the hook fires for ALL projects/directories. The plan says the hook uses 'cat .adjutant/PRIME.md 2>/dev/null || true' which is a silent no-op when the file doesn't exist, so this is technically safe. But:

1. It runs an unnecessary shell command on every Claude Code session start for non-adjutant projects
2. If a user has many hooks with empty matchers, startup gets slower
3. The spec says hooks should be project-specific but the implementation is global

Consider using a project-path matcher or at least documenting why the empty matcher is intentional.

File: checks.ts:116 (HOOK_COMMAND), spec.md edge cases section
Severity: Low - functional but wasteful for non-adjutant projects
close_reason: By design: empty matcher is correct for global hooks

title: Design: Rename ambiguous trait labels for non-technical clarity
description: Several trait names in the spec are ambiguous or overlap. Recommended renames for clarity: (1) 'architecture_focus' -> 'SYSTEM DESIGN' — 'architecture' is jargon. (2) 'modular_architecture' -> 'MODULARITY' — the word 'architecture' appears twice in the trait list, which is confusing. (3) 'qa_scalability' -> 'SCALE TESTING' — 'QA: Scalability' reads as a subcategory, not a personality trait. (4) 'qa_correctness' -> 'CORRECTNESS' — shorter, punchier. (5) 'testing_unit' -> 'UNIT TESTS' (6) 'testing_acceptance' -> 'E2E TESTS' — 'acceptance tests' is enterprise jargon most users won't know. (7) 'business_objectives' -> 'BIZ VALUE' — more approachable. (8) 'technical_depth' -> 'DEEP TECH'. Each slider label should be uppercase monospace (per theme rules) and fit in ~14 characters max to avoid wrapping on mobile. Add a brief tooltip/hint text under each label visible on hover (web) or long-press (iOS).
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: shouldDecayConfidence always returns true because lastValidatedAt is never set
description: The memory-reviewer's shouldDecayConfidence function (line 260) checks if learning.lastValidatedAt is null and returns true for decay. However, lastValidatedAt is NEVER SET anywhere in the codebase. Neither reinforceLearning (which only updates confidence, reinforcement_count, updated_at) nor insertLearning nor any other code path ever calls updateLearning with lastValidatedAt. Result: every learning will ALWAYS be decayed during weekly review, including freshly created and recently reinforced ones. Fix: either (a) set lastValidatedAt when reinforceLearning is called, or (b) change shouldDecayConfidence to check updatedAt instead. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/memory-reviewer.ts lines 259-266, backend/src/services/adjutant/memory-store.ts lines 248-254
close_reason: Fixed: shouldDecayConfidence falls back to updatedAt

title: Blockquote containing list/code renders as EmptyView

In MarkdownTextView.blockquoteContent(), non-paragraph/heading blocks inside blockquotes render as EmptyView() (line 120-122). This means if a blockquote contains a list, code block, or horizontal rule, those elements are silently dropped. Real spec files have patterns like:

> **NOTE: Write these tests FIRST**
> - Item one
> - Item two

The list items inside the blockquote would be invisible.

title: Bug: resource leak on non-initialize POST without session ID
description: POST /mcp without mcp-session-id header where body is NOT an initialize request creates orphaned transport+server. The route handler creates a new transport for any POST without session ID, but if the body isn't an initialize request, the SDK returns 400 and the transport/server are never cleaned up (no session initialized, not in connections map). Fix: check req.body.method === 'initialize' before calling createSessionTransport, or add cleanup on the error path.

title: Bug: No UNIQUE constraint on adjutant_retrospectives.session_date allows duplicate daily retros
description: Found in adj-053.1.1 (migration). The adjutant_retrospectives table has no UNIQUE constraint on session_date. If the session-retrospective behavior fires twice on the same day (e.g., server restart, race condition), duplicate retrospective rows will be created. This violates the spec's intent of 'daily' retrospectives and will confuse downstream consumers (memory-reviewer, REST API). Reproduction: INSERT two rows with session_date='2026-03-08' -- both succeed. Expected: UNIQUE constraint on session_date, or at minimum the behavior layer should handle this. Recommend adding UNIQUE constraint at schema level as defense in depth.
close_reason: Migration 013 adds UNIQUE constraint on session_date

title: Inline bold/italic cannot nest within each other
description: The MarkdownInline model represents bold, italic, boldItalic, and code as flat string-only cases (e.g. .bold(String)). This means you cannot have bold containing italic or vice versa. For example: **Bold text with *nested italic* inside** should render bold with an italic subsection, but the parser treats each as an atomic string. The inline parser scans for *** first (boldItalic), then ** (bold), then * (italic) but does not allow nesting. Real spec patterns like **bold with `code`** also break since bold wraps a string, not a recursive inline array.

title: Bug: writeAgentFile should throw when project path doesn't exist
description: If the target project path doesn't exist on disk, writeAgentFile should throw a clear error rather than silently failing or creating directories in unexpected locations. mkdir({recursive:true}) on a nonexistent base path would create the entire tree, which is wrong — the project directory itself should already exist, only .claude/agents/ should be created. QA test case added.
close_reason: Acceptable: mkdir recursive is standard Node.js pattern for ensuring directory exists

title: Bug: Work-assigner idle agent check doesn't filter stale/ghost agent profiles
description: The work-assigner shouldAct guard checks 'at least one idle agent exists in agent profiles.' But agent profiles in the state store are never deleted — once an agent connects and later disconnects, its profile remains in adjutant_agent_profiles forever. The agent-lifecycle behavior sets lastStatus='disconnected' but the profile still exists. If an agent went idle, then disconnected, then the server restarted, its profile still shows lastStatus='idle' (if it went idle before disconnecting and the disconnect event wasn't processed). The work-assigner would consider this ghost agent as an available idle agent and try to assign work to it. The agent would never receive the assignment. Additionally, the shouldAct guard should verify that 'idle' agents are also currently connected (connectedAt > disconnectedAt, or disconnectedAt IS NULL). Without this, the system will attempt assignments to dead agents, waste bd CLI calls, and leave beads in limbo (assigned but no one working). Fix: Add connection liveness check to the idle agent filter: agent.lastStatus === 'idle' AND (agent.disconnectedAt === null OR agent.connectedAt > agent.disconnectedAt).
close_reason: Fixed: work-assigner filters agents requiring lastStatus=idle, connectedAt!=null, disconnectedAt=null

title: QA: idle-proposal-nudge needs error handling for ProposalStore query failures
description: buildScheduleReason() queries ProposalStore for pending/dismissed proposals. If the SQLite query throws (DB unavailable, corruption), the behavior has no error handling spec. Should degrade gracefully: schedule the check with a note that proposal context was unavailable, rather than propagating the error and losing the nudge entirely.
close_reason: Acknowledged: follows existing pattern (self-improver has no try/catch either). Can be addressed as future improvement.

(2) A small 4-point radar/diamond chart showing the persona's top 4 category strengths (Engineering, Quality, Product, Craft) as a fingerprint — this immediately communicates specialization at a glance without reading text. (3) The persona name in uppercase monospace. (4) A short description (1 line, ellipsized). (5) A 'DEPLOY' button styled as a CRT-themed bordered rectangle. On tap, show a brief confirmation sheet with project selector (reuse SpawnAgentSheet pattern), then spawn. The radar chart on web uses inline SVG; on iOS use a Shape path. Standby cards sort below running agents but above offline agents.

If the decisions table grows large and a caller passes -1 or a very large number, this could consume excessive memory. Add bounds validation: clamp limit to [0, 1000] range or similar sensible maximum.

title: Bug: listDirectory does not verify path is a directory before calling readdirSync

This is caught by the generic catch block and returns INTERNAL_ERROR with the raw Node error message, which is not user-friendly. The iOS client would show a cryptic error. Fix: add a statSync check to verify isDirectory() before calling readdirSync, and return a clear VALIDATION_ERROR like 'Path is not a directory: README.md'. The tests also don't cover this case.

title: Bug: supersedeLearning allows circular supersede chains
description: Found in adj-053.1.2 (memory-store.ts). The supersedeLearning(oldId, newId) method does not check if the new learning is already superseded by the old one. Calling supersedeLearning(1, 2) then supersedeLearning(2, 1) creates a circular chain where both learnings reference each other via superseded_by. This breaks: (1) queryLearnings with includeSuperseded=false would exclude BOTH, (2) any code that walks the supersede chain would loop forever, (3) semantic meaning is broken — a learning can't be superseded by one it itself supersedes. Fix: add a guard in supersedeLearning to check if newId is already superseded by oldId, and throw or no-op.
close_reason: supersedeLearning detects circular chains by walking the supersede graph

title: QA: Debounce reset mechanism for idle-proposal-nudge is underspecified
description: The spec says "debounce resets after the agent transitions through a non-idle state" but does not define HOW. The behavior only triggers on agent:status_changed. When agent goes working->idle->working->idle, the debounce key must be cleared on the working transition or the second idle will never trigger a scheduleCheck. Three possible approaches: (1) behavior clears meta key on non-idle events in act(), (2) timestamp-based expiry, (3) idle-period identifier. Without clarification, implementation may get this wrong and agents will only ever get one nudge per lifetime.
close_reason: Fixed: shouldAct allows non-idle events through, act() clears debounce on non-idle transitions

title: Bug: Race condition - user navigates tab before DataSyncService has fetched data
description:
The startup flow is:
1. AdjutantApp creates ContentView
2. ContentView creates MainTabView (if not onboarding)
3. MainTabView creates AppCoordinator (@StateObject) with default tab = .overview
4. TabContent renders ALL 8 tab views eagerly
5. Each view accesses its ViewModel which may subscribe to DataSyncService

But DataSyncService.startEventStream() is only called when scene phase transitions to .active (in AdjutantApp's onChange handler). This means:
- Views are created before SSE starts
- Views subscribe to DataSyncService (triggering polling) but SSE isn't active yet
- If user taps a different tab before data arrives, they see empty content

The race: Views display cached data (from ResponseCache), but if cache is empty (fresh install), the user sees completely empty views on ALL tabs simultaneously until the first polling cycle completes (~30 seconds for 'efficient' priority, ~120 seconds for 'pollingOnly').

UX impact: On fresh install, all tabs show empty/loading state for up to 30+ seconds.

Suggested fix:
- Trigger an initial data fetch (refreshAll) immediately on app launch, not just on tab subscription
- Show explicit loading indicators when no cached data exists AND fetch is pending
- Consider showing the skeleton UI (adj-049.3.1) as the loading state

title: Settings theme picker: preview section uses hardcoded backgrounds and lacks scheme-specific differentiation

Users cannot see what each scheme actually looks like before selecting it. (2) The ThemeButton component only shows a colored circle and label text — it does not show a representative mini-preview of each scheme (e.g., background color, text sample, effect style). All three scheme selectors look nearly identical except for the accent dot color. A user seeing three colored circles cannot understand the dramatic difference between a dark CRT terminal, a clean white document view, and a purple sci-fi interface. FIX: (1) Replace hardcoded background with theme-aware background. (2) Make each ThemeButton show a mini card preview with the scheme's actual background color, primary text color, and a hint of its personality (e.g., scanline texture for Pip-Boy, clean white for Document, subtle purple gradient for StarCraft). This is the first place users interact with the theme system — it needs to sell each scheme.

title: CRTCard uses hardcoded CRTTheme.Background.panel — breaks Document and StarCraft schemes

In StarCraft mode, cards will show green-tinted backgrounds instead of purple-tinted ones. CRTCard is used throughout the entire app (Settings, Dashboard widgets, Proposals, etc.), making this one of the most visible issues. Additionally, the corner bracket decorations (CornerBracket) are inappropriate for Document mode — they should be hidden or replaced with simple borders. FIX: Replace CRTTheme.Background.panel with theme.background.panel (from the environment-injected theme). Conditionally hide corner brackets when theme.crtEffectsEnabled is false.

title: Document scheme: insufficient contrast between dim text (#888888) and white background
description: The Document scheme uses #888888 (rgb 0.533,0.533,0.533) for dim/secondary text on a #FFFFFF white background. The contrast ratio is approximately 3.54:1, which fails WCAG AA for normal text (requires 4.5:1) and passes only for large text (3:1). This dim color is used heavily throughout the app for: captions, secondary labels, header badges, help text in Settings, dim status indicators, and inactive tab bar labels. Users with any degree of visual impairment will struggle to read this text. The Pip-Boy and StarCraft schemes do not have this issue because their dim colors sit on very dark backgrounds. FIX: Darken the Document dim color from #888888 to #666666 (contrast ratio ~5.74:1, passing WCAG AA). Alternatively, use the system secondary label color (.secondary) which is already optimized for the platform.
close_reason: Fixed: bumped Document dim from #888888 to #666666 for WCAG AA compliance

title: Bug: NetworkMonitor initial state is optimistic (isConnected=true) before first path update
description:
NetworkMonitor initializes with isConnected = true as the default value. The NWPathMonitor pathUpdateHandler fires asynchronously on a background DispatchQueue after startMonitoring() is called.

There is a window between init and the first pathUpdateHandler callback where:
1. isConnected is true (default)
2. connectionType is .unknown (default)
3. No actual network check has been performed

Any code that checks NetworkMonitor.shared.isConnected during this window will get an optimistic 'true' even if the device has no connectivity. This affects:
- BackgroundTaskService.performRefresh() which gates on NetworkMonitor.shared.isConnected
- AppState.setupNetworkRecoveryObserver() which subscribes to NetworkMonitor.shared.$isConnected.dropFirst()

The .dropFirst() in AppState's observer is particularly problematic: if the first real update says 'disconnected', the dropFirst catches it but the recovery logic only fires on transitions to true. If the initial real state is 'disconnected', the user sees isNetworkAvailable = true in AppState (never updated because the handler only triggers on transitions).

Suggested fix:
- Initialize isConnected to false (conservative default)
- Or synchronously query NWPathMonitor.currentPath in init before starting async updates
- Remove the .dropFirst() on the network observer since the first update carries meaningful state

title: Bug: assignment_count grows monotonically — no decrement on unassign, close, or rebalance
description: The spec adds assignment_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0 to agent profiles 'for load balancing.' The work-assigner increments it on assignment. But nothing decrements it when: (1) A bead is closed by the assigned agent. (2) A bead is unassigned by the work-rebalancer. (3) A bead is reassigned by the user. This means assignment_count grows forever and becomes useless for load balancing — an agent that completed 50 beads looks overloaded compared to a new agent with 0 assignments, even if both are currently idle with no active work. If assignment_count is meant for 'how many beads are currently assigned to this agent', it should be computed dynamically (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM beads WHERE assignee=agent AND status=in_progress) rather than stored as a monotonic counter. If it's meant for 'total historical assignments', it's fine but shouldn't be used for load balancing without also tracking completions. Fix: Either (a) compute current load dynamically from beads data, (b) add a completion_count field and use assignment_count - completion_count for current load, or (c) rename to total_assignments and document that it's not for load balancing.
close_reason: Accepted: assignment_count is used for affinity heuristics, not precise accounting. Monotonic increment is sufficient for load-balancing priority. Decrementing would add complexity with no clear benefit.

title: Bug: Null byte injection not checked in file path validation

On some Node.js/OS combinations, a null byte in the path can truncate the string at the OS level while Node's path.resolve sees the full string. This could bypass the startsWith check. Fix: reject any relativePath containing null bytes before passing to resolve(). Example: relativePath = 'safe/path\x00../../etc/passwd'

title: Bug: Work-assigner bd calls will contend with MCP bd mutex causing latency spikes
description: There are TWO serialization mechanisms for bd access: (1) BdSemaphore in bd-client.ts (maxConcurrency=1) used by all execBd calls. (2) SimpleMutex in mcp-tools/beads.ts used by all MCP bead tool calls. The work-assigner will add frequent bd CLI calls to the serialization queue: bd ready --json (every 5 minutes minimum, plus on every agent:status_changed and bead:closed event), and bd update --assignee (for each assignment). Each bd CLI call takes ~100-500ms. If multiple agents are reporting status changes rapidly (e.g. during team standup), the work-assigner will queue bd ready calls that compete with agents' own bd operations (list_beads, show_bead, create_bead, update_bead, close_bead). This can cause latency spikes for agents waiting to interact with beads. The 5-minute cron sweep will always queue at least one bd ready call, but event-triggered calls can pile up. Fix: (a) The work-assigner should cache the bd ready results with a short TTL (e.g. 10 seconds) to avoid redundant queries. (b) Consider reading the beads database directly with SQLite instead of shelling out to bd CLI for read-only queries, bypassing the semaphore entirely. (c) At minimum, batch event-triggered checks — if 5 agents go idle within 2 seconds, run bd ready once, not 5 times.
close_reason: Accepted: P3 — bd semaphore contention is bounded by the existing BdSemaphore(1) serialization. Work-assigner runs at most once per 30s debounce. Latency spikes are acceptable at this frequency.

title: Bug: ResponseCache.persistChatMessages debounce task creates retain cycle risk
description:
ResponseCache.persistChatMessages() creates a Task with [weak self] that captures 'messages' by value:

  persistDebounceTask = Task { [weak self] in
      try? await Task.sleep(nanoseconds: ...)
      guard !Task.isCancelled else { return }
      self?.performPersist(messages)
  }

Issues:
1. The 'messages' array is captured by value in the Task closure. If there are many messages (up to 50 PersistentMessage objects), this creates a copy that lives for the debounce duration (5 seconds).
2. If updateChatMessages is called rapidly, each call cancels the previous Task but the cancelled Task's copy of 'messages' isn't immediately deallocated - it waits for the Task.sleep to complete or be cancelled.
3. The persistDebounceTask property holds a strong reference to the Task. If the singleton is accessed during init cascade, this could interact with Swift concurrency's actor isolation in unexpected ways.

Also: The check 'if now.timeIntervalSince(lastPersistTime) >= Self.persistDebounceInterval' allows immediate persistence on the FIRST call but then debounces subsequent calls. This means the first call after 5+ seconds of quiet always persists immediately, but rapid-fire updates during initial data load could skip the first persist window and delay by 5 seconds.

Impact: Memory spikes during rapid message updates + delayed persistence during initial load.

Suggested fix: Use Combine's .debounce operator instead of manual Task-based debouncing to avoid the retain/memory issues.

title: Add device-token-service unit tests

title: Bug: No CHECK constraint on confidence column allows out-of-range values
description: Found in adj-053.1.1 (migration). The adjutant_learnings table has no CHECK constraint on the confidence column (REAL type). Values outside [0.0, 1.0] can be inserted without error. The spec says confidence should be capped at 1.0, but nothing in the schema prevents negative values or values > 1.0. The CRUD layer (adj-053.1.2) will need to enforce this, but a schema-level CHECK constraint would be a defense-in-depth measure. Reproduction: INSERT INTO adjutant_learnings (category, topic, content, source_type, confidence) VALUES ('test', 'test', 'test', 'test', 999.0) -- succeeds but shouldn't. Expected: CHECK(confidence >= 0.0 AND confidence <= 1.0) on the column.
close_reason: Migration 013 adds CHECK constraint for confidence BETWEEN 0.0 AND 1.0

title: Bug: AppDelegate.activateAudioSessionForBackground called on every remote notification
description:
AppDelegate.handleRemoteNotification() calls activateAudioSessionForBackground() on EVERY remote notification, regardless of type:

  @MainActor
  private func handleRemoteNotification(userInfo: ...) async -> UIBackgroundFetchResult {
      activateAudioSessionForBackground()  // Called first, always
      ...
      switch type {
      case 'mail', 'new_mail': ...
      case 'task', 'bead_update': ...
      case 'chat_message': ...  // This type doesn't need audio
      case 'system': ...
      }
  }

activateAudioSessionForBackground() calls:
  AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().setCategory(.playback, mode: .spokenAudio, ...)
  AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().setActive(true)

This is an expensive operation that:
1. Interrupts other audio playback on the device
2. Takes control of the audio session even for chat_message notifications that don't need audio
3. Can cause a visible/audible glitch if the user is listening to music

Impact: Audio interruption on every push notification, even for silent chat messages.

Suggested fix: Only activate the audio session when the notification actually needs TTS playback (mail and bead types with voice enabled). Move the call into handleMailNotification and handleTaskNotification only, guarded by !AppState.shared.isVoiceMuted.

title: QA: .mcp.json uses env var interpolation that Claude Code may not support
description:
.mcp.json contains:

  "X-Agent-Id": "${ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID:-unknown}"

This shell-style variable interpolation (${VAR:-default}) may not be supported by Claude Code's MCP configuration parser. Claude Code likely reads .mcp.json as plain JSON, treating the value as a literal string '${ADJUTANT_AGENT_ID:-unknown}' rather than performing shell expansion.

If this is intentional (the MCP server receives the literal string and handles it server-side), then it's fine. But if the expectation is that the agent ID is resolved client-side before connecting, this will silently fail and all agents will connect with the literal string as their ID.

The init command should document or verify how this interpolation works.

File: .mcp.json:7
Severity: Medium - could cause all agents to have the same ID, breaking identity resolution
close_reason: Doc/plan out of date. Not a code issue.

title: Bug: Singleton initialization cascade blocks main thread on cold launch
description:
AppState.shared is a lazy static singleton marked @MainActor. When ContentView.init() accesses AppState.shared, it triggers the entire init chain synchronously on the main thread:

1. AppState.init() calls loadPersistedState() which makes 6+ synchronous UserDefaults reads
2. loadPersistedState() also sets up 6 Combine 059CDEJNQRTWXghlsubscriptions with .sink
3. AppState.init() calls setupNetworkRecoveryObserver() which accesses NetworkMonitor.shared (another singleton init)
4. NetworkMonitor.init() creates NWPathMonitor and calls startMonitoring() - starts monitoring on a background queue
5. AppState.init() calls registerDependencies() which accesses DependencyContainer.shared (another singleton init)

The net effect: accessing AppState.shared triggers 3 cascading singleton initializations all on the main thread before the first frame renders. Each init involves UserDefaults I/O and Combine subscription setup.

Impact: Estimated 200-400ms of main thread blocking before any UI renders on cold launch.

Suggested fix: Break the cascade. AppState.init() should only do the bare minimum (load theme + API URL) and defer NetworkMonitor access, Combine subscriptions, and dependency registration to a post-render .start() method.
close_reason: Fixed by Phase 1+2: singleton cascade broken up via deferred init and background loading

title: QA: mode-service.ts, mode route, and ModeContext need removal/simplification

- backend/src/services/mode-service.ts — switchMode(), getModeInfo() with gastown features
- backend/src/routes/mode.ts — POST /api/mode endpoint for switching
- frontend/src/contexts/ModeContext.tsx — isGasTown/isSwarm flags, switchMode, useVisibleTabs
- frontend/src/hooks/useDeploymentMode.ts — legacy mode detection hook

These should either be:
1. Simplified to always return 'swarm' mode (minimal change, backward compat)
2. Completely removed (cleaner but more frontend changes)

The ModeContext provides useVisibleTabs() which controls tab visibility — removing the context requires updating all components that use useMode(). Option 1 is safer for a first pass.

Headers get larger font, code blocks get a background panel, lists get bullet prefixes, blockquotes get a left bar, inline bold/italic/code use AttributedString. Must respect all 4 CRT themes.

title: Bug: ContentView creates redundant DependencyContainer instance as @StateObject
description:
ContentView creates:
  @StateObject private var dependencyContainer = DependencyContainer()

This creates a NEW DependencyContainer instance separate from DependencyContainer.shared. The @StateObject means SwiftUI owns its lifecycle. This instance is passed into MainTabView via .environmentObject(dependencyContainer).

However, all actual dependency resolution in the app uses DependencyContainer.shared (e.g., AppState.registerDependencies(), BeadStatusMonitor.init() resolving TTS service, the @Injected property wrapper).

This means:
1. Two DependencyContainer instances exist
2. The one injected into the environment has nothing registered in it
3. Any view that uses @EnvironmentObject to access DependencyContainer gets the empty one
4. All actual dependency resolution uses the .shared singleton directly

This is confusing architecture but not currently broken because no view appears to use @EnvironmentObject for DependencyContainer to resolve dependencies. The MainTabView has @EnvironmentObject DependencyContainer but only for ObservableObject conformance, not for resolving.

Impact: Unnecessary object creation on startup + architectural confusion.

Suggested fix:
- Remove the @StateObject DependencyContainer from ContentView
- Or make ContentView use DependencyContainer.shared
- Audit whether any views actually need DependencyContainer as an environment object

title: QA: getVersion() in cli/index.ts resolves wrong package.json path when compiled
description:
In cli/index.ts:24, getVersion() reads package.json relative to __dirname:

  readFileSync(join(__dirname, '..', 'package.json'), 'utf-8')

When compiled, __dirname is dist/cli/, so '..' resolves to dist/, not the project root. The package.json is at the project root, not in dist/. This means the version lookup will fail silently (caught by try/catch) and return 'unknown'.

The bin/cli.js entry point has a similar line (line 13) but uses join(__dirname, '..', 'package.json') where __dirname is bin/, so '..' correctly resolves to the project root.

For cli/index.ts compiled to dist/cli/index.js, it should be join(__dirname, '..', '..', 'package.json') to reach the project root.

File: cli/index.ts:24
Severity: Medium - 'adjutant --version' shows 'unknown' when run from compiled dist
close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy

title: Bug: EventStreamService.runStream creates new URLSession on every reconnect
description:
EventStreamService.runStream() creates a new URLSession on every call:

  let sessionConfig = URLSessionConfiguration.default
  sessionConfig.timeoutIntervalForRequest = 60
  sessionConfig.timeoutIntervalForResource = 0
  let session = URLSession(configuration: sessionConfig)

This is called on every connection attempt (including reconnects). URLSession creation is not free - it allocates a thread pool and internal state. On repeated reconnect cycles (e.g., server is down, exponential backoff triggers 5-10 reconnection attempts), 5-10 URLSession instances are created without being invalidated.

URLSession objects leak if not invalidated with invalidateAndCancel() or finishTasksAndInvalidate(). The GC may eventually clean them up, but until then they hold onto their delegate, configuration, and internal dispatch queues.

Impact: Resource leak during reconnection cycles. Each reconnect creates ~200KB of session overhead that isn't released promptly.

Suggested fix:
- Create a single URLSession in init and reuse it
- Or invalidate the previous session before creating a new one
- Store the session reference and call invalidateAndCancel() in stop()

title: Bug: 009-adjutant-state.sql missing index on adjutant_agent_profiles.last_status

With many agents this is minor, but for the stale-agent-nudger behavior that will run on schedule, an index on last_status or last_status_at would be beneficial. title: Automated TestFlight Release Pipeline via Git Workflows
description:
Set up end-to-end CI/CD git workflows to automatically build, sign, and release new TestFlight builds for the l2rr2l iOS app (Bundle ID: com.jmm.l2rr2l).

Key areas:

1. **CI/CD Pipeline Setup** — Configure GitHub Actions (or equivalent) workflows triggered on pushes/merges to the appropriate branch(es) to build the iOS app.

2. **Code Signing & Provisioning** — Set up Apple certificates, provisioning profiles, and securely store credentials in CI secrets. Configure automatic signing for TestFlight distribution.

3. **Build & Archive** — Automate xcodebuild archive and export steps targeting the com.jmm.l2rr2l bundle identifier.

4. **TestFlight Upload** — Integrate App Store Connect API or altool/xcrun to automatically upload builds to TestFlight after successful archive.

5. **Versioning & Build Numbers** — Implement automatic build number incrementing to avoid collisions on TestFlight uploads.

6. **Notifications & Status** — Add build status badges, Slack/webhook notifications for successful/failed builds.

Deliverable: A working GitHub Actions workflow that produces TestFlight builds automatically on merge to the release branch.

title: Session output leaking into normal chat view
description: After sending a message, terminal session output from agent tmux sessions appears in the normal chat view. Chat should only display SQLite-backed messages (chat_message type). Investigate: (1) WS handleMessage broadcasts type:'message' (not chat_message) — CommunicationContext.tsx handles both types on line 252-253. (2) Check if session_output events are somehow being broadcast via wsBroadcast. (3) iOS ChatWebSocketService handleLegacyChatMessage may be too permissive. (4) The WS message path should persist to SQLite first, then broadcast as chat_message — currently it only broadcasts without persistence.
close_reason: Fixed session output leak: WS messages now route through SQLite message store and broadcast as chat_message. Frontend/iOS only handle chat_message type.

title: bd sync commits pollute git history
description: 91/1416 commits (6.4%) are 'chore: bd sync' — pure beads metadata commits with no code changes. Root cause: agents run bd close/update AFTER their code commit, creating a timing gap. The pre-commit hook auto-stages .beads/issues.jsonl, but by the time beads change, the code commit is already done. The pre-push hook then blocks until a separate sync commit is created. Options: (1) pre-push auto-commit instead of blocking — reduces but doesn't eliminate pollution, masks errors, disconnects beads changes from context. (2) Fix agent workflow ordering — run bd close/update BEFORE git commit so beads changes ride along for free. (3) Proposal to bd CLI for deferred export mode. Option 2 is the cleanest fix but requires discipline in spawn prompts and PRIME.md.

title: Bug: superseded_by FK has no ON DELETE behavior — orphaned references possible
description: Found in adj-053.1.1 (migration). The adjutant_learnings.superseded_by column references adjutant_learnings(id) but has no ON DELETE clause. With foreign_keys=ON (set in database.ts), if a superseding learning is deleted, the old learning's superseded_by will still reference a non-existent row. SQLite's default FK behavior is RESTRICT/NO ACTION, meaning deletes of superseding rows will fail with a FK constraint error. This blocks pruneStale() from deleting superseding learnings if the superseded rows still exist. Either: (a) add ON DELETE SET NULL, or (b) document that prune must handle the supersede chain correctly by deleting in the right order.
close_reason: Migration 013 adds ON DELETE SET NULL for superseded_by FK

title: Dashboard batch: silent error swallowing hides all fetch failures
description: In DashboardViewModel.fetchDashboard() (lines 104-111), the catch block is empty: 'catch { // Keep existing data on error }'. This means any network failure, decode failure, or server error is completely invisible. There is no error state set on BaseViewModel (no 'errorMessage' assignment), no isRefreshing=false in the catch path... wait — isRefreshing IS set to false in refresh() after fetchDashboard() returns, so that part is OK. However, ALL section-level errors from the backend (DashboardSection.error fields) are also silently ignored: processDashboardResponse() checks section.data \!= nil but never logs or surfaces section.error strings. Users have no way to know if beads, mail, crew, or status sections failed independently. File: ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 104-111 and processDashboardResponse() lines 114-151.
close_reason: Added CancellationError handling and error surfacing via handleError.

title: Refactor: FTS5 search query not sanitized for special characters
description: In message-store.ts searchMessages(), the query string is passed directly to FTS5 MATCH. FTS5 has special syntax characters (AND, OR, NOT, quotes, asterisks, etc.) that can cause parse errors if user/agent input contains them. For example, searching for 'C++ OR crash' would be interpreted as FTS5 boolean logic. While this is not SQL injection (parameterized queries prevent that), it causes unhandled SQLite exceptions that will 500 the REST API and crash MCP tool calls. Fix: escape or sanitize the FTS5 query input, or wrap the search in try/catch with a user-friendly error.

title: Bug: Agent spawner behavior needs concurrency guard like work-assigner
description:
The spec for adj-052.2.3 defines agent-spawner as both event-triggered (bead:created, bead:assigned) and scheduled (*/10 cron). The work-assigner behavior (work-assigner.ts:68-69) already has a concurrency guard (`let assigning = false`) to prevent double-execution when the cron and an event fire simultaneously. The agent-spawner behavior MUST have an equivalent guard.

Without this guard, the following race is possible:
1. bead:created event fires, agent-spawner starts evaluating (sees 0 idle agents, 4 active, max=5)
2. */10 cron fires, agent-spawner starts evaluating (same state: 0 idle, 4 active, max=5)
3. Both decide to spawn => 2 agents created, hitting max=6, or hitting the TOCTOU in LifecycleManager

Additionally, the spawn_cooldown_ms config needs to be checked atomically — the pattern of reading state.getMeta('last-spawn-at'), comparing timestamps, then setting it after spawn has a gap where two concurrent calls can both pass the cooldown check.

**Impact**: P2 — double-spawn wastes resources and may exceed agent cap. The cooldown bypass means spawns happen faster than intended.

**Fix**: Add a boolean concurrency guard (like `let spawning = false`) in the agent-spawner factory closure. Explicitly document that spawn_cooldown_ms must be checked in shouldAct() and updated atomically in act().

**Files**: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-spawner.ts (to be created per adj-052.2.3)
close_reason: Fixed: agent-spawner uses in-memory boolean lock to prevent concurrent spawns

title: Bug: ContentView.init accesses AppState.shared synchronously, triggering full init cascade
description:
ContentView.init() contains:
  _showOnboarding = State(initialValue: !AppState.shared.isOnboardingComplete)

This is the FIRST access to AppState.shared in the app lifecycle (triggered when AdjutantApp.body creates ContentView()). This single property access triggers the ENTIRE singleton initialization cascade:

1. AppState.shared -> AppState.init()
2. -> loadPersistedBaseURL() (UserDefaults read)
3. -> loadPersistedAPIKey() (UserDefaults read)
4. -> APIClient creation
5. -> loadPersistedState() (4 more UserDefaults reads + 6 Combine subscriptions)
6. -> setupNetworkRecoveryObserver() -> NetworkMonitor.shared -> NWPathMonitor init
7. -> registerDependencies() -> DependencyContainer.shared init

All of this happens during a View.init(), which is called during SwiftUI's body evaluation. SwiftUI expects View.init() to be fast and cheap.

The isOnboardingComplete computed property itself is lightweight (just checks the API URL host), but the side effect of triggering AppState.shared init is the real cost.

Additionally, ContentView uses @ObservedObject (not @StateObject) for appState:
  @ObservedObject private var appState = AppState.shared

This means ContentView subscribes to ALL AppState @Published property changes. Any change to any of AppState's 11 @Published properties triggers a ContentView re-render, even if ContentView only uses currentTheme.

Suggested fix:
- Move the showOnboarding check out of init - use .onAppear or .task instead
- Use @StateObject with a lightweight ViewModel that only exposes needed state
- Or split AppState into BootstrapState (theme + URL, loaded sync) and DeferredState (rest, loaded async)
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.2.1: AppState split into bootstrap + deferred, ContentView uses .task{}

title: Refactor: REST messages router missing limit validation
description: In routes/messages.ts GET /, the limit query param is parsed with parseInt but never validated. A client could pass limit=999999999 and pull the entire message history in one request, causing memory pressure. Similarly, limit=-1 or limit=NaN (from invalid input) would cause unpredictable behavior. The POST endpoint uses Zod validation, but the GET endpoint has no input validation at all. Add Zod validation or at least clamp the limit to a reasonable range (e.g., 1-200).
close_reason: Deferred P3: limit defaults to 50 in service layer, validation is nice-to-have

title: D2: Remove agent from swarm

title: Bug: BeadStatusMonitor.loadKnownStates() UserDefaults JSON decode on main thread
description:
BeadStatusMonitor.init() calls loadKnownStates() which:
1. Reads Data from UserDefaults (key: BeadStatusMonitor.knownStates)
2. Runs JSONDecoder().decode([String: String].self, from: data)

The knownBeadStates dictionary grows unbounded - it accumulates ALL bead IDs ever seen. In a long-running project with hundreds of beads, this dictionary and its JSON serialization/deserialization becomes increasingly expensive.

Two issues:
1. JSON decode on main thread during init cascade
2. No pruning of old/closed bead states - dictionary grows forever

Impact: On a project with 500+ beads, the JSON decode of the known states dictionary could take 10-20ms. This adds to the main thread blocking during the singleton cascade triggered by AppDelegate -> BackgroundTaskService.shared -> (BeadStatusMonitor access path).

Suggested fix:
- Defer loadKnownStates() to a start() method
- Add LRU-style pruning to limit knownBeadStates to most recent N entries (e.g., 200)
- Consider moving persistence to a file rather than UserDefaults for large data

title: Bug: initMcpServer creates a second MessageStore instance
description: initMcpServer() in mcp-server.ts creates its own MessageStore (line 77: const store = createMessageStore(db)), while index.ts also creates one (const messageStore = ...) and passes it to registerMessagingTools. This means status tools (set_status, report_progress, announce) operate on a DIFFERENT MessageStore instance than messaging tools (send_message, read_messages). While both use the same underlying SQLite DB, they hold different prepared statements. This duplication is confusing and will cause bugs if any caching or state is ever added to MessageStore. Fix: pass the message store into initMcpServer() instead of creating one internally, or move all tool registration to a single place.

title: Bug: last_epic_id column semantics are undefined — spec doesn't explain how it's set or used
description: The state store migration adds last_epic_id TEXT to agent profiles, and the spec says it's for 'epic affinity' in the matching algorithm ('prefer agents that previously worked on the same epic'). But the spec doesn't define: (1) What counts as an 'epic' — is it the bead's parent epic? What if the bead has multiple parent epics in a hierarchy? What if the bead has no parent epic? (2) When is it set — on assignment? On bead close? On status change? (3) What happens when an agent works on beads from multiple epics — only the last one is stored, so affinity is lost for earlier epics. (4) How the matching algorithm uses it — does the work-assigner need to look up each candidate bead's parent epic and compare to each idle agent's last_epic_id? This requires an additional bd show call per bead to get its dependencies/parent, which is expensive given bd semaphore serialization. (5) Most beads in this project use hierarchical IDs (adj-052.1.3 is a child of adj-052.1). Can epic affinity be inferred from the ID prefix instead of a separate column? Fix: Define the semantics clearly before implementation. Suggest storing the bead ID prefix (e.g. 'adj-052.1') as the epic affinity key, setting it on assignment, and using prefix matching for affinity scoring.
close_reason: Resolved: last_epic_id is set by work-assigner when assigning beads — tracks the parent epic of the most recently assigned bead for affinity matching.

title: Bug: DataSyncService eager SSE + Combine setup blocks startup
description:
DataSyncService.shared is lazily initialized on first access, but its init() does significant work:

1. loadFromCache() - accesses ResponseCache.shared (triggering its init cascade)
2. setupSSEIntegration() - creates two Combine subscriptions on EventStreamService.shared (another singleton init)
3. observeCommunicationPriority() - accesses AppState.shared.communicationPriority and creates a Combine subscription

The SSE integration setup accesses EventStreamService.shared, which doesn't do much in init but adds another singleton to the cascade. The Combine subscriptions (eventStream.$state, eventStream.eventSubject, AppState.shared.$communicationPriority) are set up eagerly even though SSE isn't started until .active scene phase.

Impact: ~50-100ms of unnecessary init work during startup for services that won't be needed until after the first frame renders.

Note: DataSyncService is first accessed from AdjutantApp.body's onChange(of: scenePhase) handler, which means it's triggered during SwiftUI's first body evaluation.

Suggested fix: Make DataSyncService.init() a no-op. Move loadFromCache(), setupSSEIntegration(), and observeCommunicationPriority() into an explicit start() method called after the first frame renders.
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.1.2 + adj-049.2.3: DataSyncService deferred + Combine setup in start()

title: Bug: Deep link via notification tap before AppCoordinator subscription setup
description:
AppCoordinator.init() checks NotificationService.shared.pendingDeepLinkAgentId and sets up NotificationCenter observers for .navigateToChat, .navigateToMail, .navigateToTask.

However, AppCoordinator is created as a @StateObject in MainTabView (which is created after ContentView decides showOnboarding is false). If a notification tap fires BEFORE MainTabView renders (e.g., user taps a notification to cold-launch the app), the deep link flow is:

1. NotificationService.didReceive sets pendingDeepLinkAgentId and posts .navigateToChat
2. But AppCoordinator doesn't exist yet - MainTabView hasn't been created
3. The NotificationCenter post is lost (no subscribers)
4. When AppCoordinator.init() runs later, it checks pendingDeepLinkAgentId (this part works)

The .navigateToChat notification post is wasted, but pendingDeepLinkAgentId serves as a fallback. However, if the user has the onboarding screen showing (showOnboarding = true), neither MainTabView nor AppCoordinator ever gets created during that session, and the deep link is silently dropped.

Also: .navigateToTask and .navigateToMail have NO fallback mechanism (no pending state) - those notification taps are completely lost on cold start if they arrive before AppCoordinator.init().

Suggested fix:
1. Move pending deep link state to a shared location (e.g., AppState) so it persists across view hierarchy changes
2. Add pendingDeepLinkTaskId and pendingDeepLinkMailId similar to pendingDeepLinkAgentId
3. Make AppCoordinator check all pending deep links on init, not just chat
